title: Apply: Federalism
description: Apply your knowledge about federalism to this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is the Tenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.**

The powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution, nor prohibited by it to the States, are reserved to the States respectively, or to the people.

**Which scenario is an example of a state policy that falls under the “powers not delegated”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Texas passes a law exempting people from having to pay federal taxes.
Florida decides to add a tariff on any goods imported from another country.
Missouri passes a law that requires all students to pass a civics test to graduate.
Arizona forms an Arizonan army to extend its border south into Mexico.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 51 by James Madison.**

In the [[☃ definition 1]] republic of America, the power surrendered by the people is first divided between two distinct [[☃ definition 2]], and then the portion allotted to each [is] subdivided among distinct and separate departments. Hence a double security arises to the rights of the people. The different governments will control each other, at the same time that each will be controlled by itself.

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: made of many different parts that are put together

choices.content: The federal system divides power between the different branches of government so that the federal government has supremacy over state laws.
The federal system provides more than one protection for individual rights, as different levels of government both check each other and self-regulate.
The federal system gives powers to both levels of government so that the power of the people is limited and they cannot replace the government.
The federal system creates two different governments with unique powers so that there is no overlap or conflict between the two levels of government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Brandeis’s dissenting opinion in *New State Ice Co. v. Liebmann* (1932).**

It is one of the [[☃ definition 1]] incidents of the federal system that a single courageous state may, if its citizens choose, serve as a laboratory; and try novel social and economic experiments without risk to the rest of the country.

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The federal system controls the political, social, and economic experiments that states run to get as much information as possible about successful policies.
The federal system requires states to work together to implement federal policies in a uniform way so that those policies are as effective as possible.
The federal system gives the federal government supremacy over state laws and allows the federal government to suppress state laws that conflict with federal law.
The federal system allows single states to experiment with new social and economic policies, providing a testing ground without affecting the entire nation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Brandeis’s dissenting opinion in *New State Ice Co. v. Liebmann* (1932).**

It is one of the [[☃ definition 1]] incidents of the federal system that a single courageous state may, if its citizens choose, serve as a laboratory; and try novel social and economic experiments without risk to the rest of the country.

**Which scenario best exemplifies the “laboratory” that Justice Brandeis describes in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Early in the COVID-19 pandemic, some states closed all schools, while others kept their schools open and allowed students to attend.
In compliance with a federal law, several states raise the minimum driving age from 16 to 18 years old.
Following the 2020 census, Idaho redraws their congressional districts to address the increase in their population.
Following a 1954 Supreme Court ruling, Mississippi integrates their schools so that students of all races can attend.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best summarizes the trend in women’s suffrage shown in the map?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Map showing the degrees of suffrage women had in each state. Women had full suffrage in 15 states before the 19th Amendment. women had presidential suffrage in Rhode Island, and presidential and municipal suffrage in North Dakota, Nebraska, and Illinois. Women had primary suffrage in Texas and Arkansas, municipal suffrage only in Vermont, and municipal suffrage in some cities in Ohio and Florida. Twelve states gave women the right to vote in school-related elections. But twelves states gave women no level of suffrage: Alabama, Georgia, Indiana, Maine, Maryland, Missouri, North Carolina, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, and West Virginia.

choices.content: Many states implemented partial or full suffrage for women even before the ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment.
Some states gave women the right to vote in state and local elections, but no states gave women full suffrage rights.
Some states agreed to ratify the Nineteenth Amendment, while other states rejected the amendment.
States on the East Coast were more likely to grant women municipal suffrage than states in the West.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best explains how the trend shown in the map was possible?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Map showing the degrees of suffrage women had in each state. Women had full suffrage in 15 states before the 19th Amendment. women had presidential suffrage in Rhode Island, and presidential and municipal suffrage in North Dakota, Nebraska, and Illinois. Women had primary suffrage in Texas and Arkansas, municipal suffrage only in Vermont, and municipal suffrage in some cities in Ohio and Florida. Twelve states gave women the right to vote in school-related elections. But twelves states gave women no level of suffrage: Alabama, Georgia, Indiana, Maine, Maryland, Missouri, North Carolina, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, and West Virginia.

choices.content: The federal government has no power over the administration of federal elections, as this power is not enumerated in the Constitution.
The amendment process requires three-fourths of the states to agree to ratify an amendment to the Constitution.
The states have a lot of power to determine the time, place, and manner of elections including who gets to vote.
The federal government asked the western states to give women suffrage as an experiment before the ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 23 by Alexander Hamilton.**

Shall the Union be [[☃ definition 1]] the guardian of the common safety? Are [[☃ definition 2]] and armies and revenues necessary to this purpose? The government of the Union must be empowered to pass all laws, and to make all regulations which have relation to them. The same must be the case in respect to commerce, and to every other matter to which its jurisdiction is permitted to extend. 

Is the administration of justice between the citizens of the same State the proper department of the local governments? These must possess all the authorities which are connected with this [[☃ definition 3]], and with every other that may be allotted to their particular [[☃ definition 4]] and direction. Not to [[☃ definition 5]] in each case a degree of power [[☃ definition 6]] to the [[☃ definition 7]], would be to violate the most obvious rules of prudence and propriety, and [[☃ definition 8]] to trust the great interests of the nation to hands which are disabled from managing them with vigor and success.

**Which statement best summarizes Hamilton’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: carelessly; without planning for the future

choices.content: The laws passed by the federal government must have supremacy over state laws; otherwise the states will ignore all federal laws.
The powers of government need to be divided so that the federal government handles national issues and the state governments handle state-specific issues.
The states must have the power to override any federal law that affects local issues, as the federal government cannot foresee the consequences of its laws.
The federal government should be responsible only for defending the country from foreign threats, and state governments should handle all other issues.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 23 by Alexander Hamilton.**

Shall the Union be [[☃ definition 1]] the guardian of the common safety? Are [[☃ definition 2]] and armies and revenues necessary to this purpose? The government of the Union must be empowered to pass all laws, and to make all regulations which have relation to them. The same must be the case in respect to commerce, and to every other matter to which its jurisdiction is permitted to extend. 

Is the administration of justice between the citizens of the same State the proper department of the local governments? These must possess all the authorities which are connected with this [[☃ definition 3]], and with every other that may be allotted to their particular [[☃ definition 4]] and direction. Not to [[☃ definition 5]] in each case a degree of power [[☃ definition 6]] to the [[☃ definition 7]], would be to violate the most obvious rules of prudence and propriety, and [[☃ definition 8]] to trust the great interests of the nation to hands which are disabled from managing them with vigor and success.

**Hamilton describes which principle of the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: carelessly; without planning for the future

choices.content: Separation of powers
Checks and balances
Individual rights
Federalism

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the list of amendments submitted to the Pennsylvania Ratifying Convention in 1787.**

That the [[☃ definition 1]], freedom and independence of the several states shall be retained, and every power, jurisdiction and right which is not by this constitution expressly [[☃ definition 2]] to the United States in Congress assembled.

**The language in this text inspired which part of the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Elections Clause
The Supremacy Clause
The Tenth Amendment
The Fourteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the list of amendments submitted to the Pennsylvania Ratifying Convention in 1787.**

That the [[☃ definition 1]], freedom and independence of the several states shall be retained, and every power, jurisdiction and right which is not by this constitution expressly [[☃ definition 2]] to the United States in Congress assembled.

**Which statement best describes how the principles in this proposed amendment would reshape the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It would allow the state governments to ignore any federal laws that conflict with state laws.
It would ensure that all powers not explicitly given to the federal government would be left to the states.
It would give the federal courts authority over certain legal issues and the state courts authority over other issues.
It would give federal laws supremacy over state laws, meaning states must follow a federal law even when it conflicts with state law.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from notes taken during the debate at the Massachusetts Ratifying Convention in 1788. The speaker is George Cabot, and he is speaking about the Elections Clause.**

. . . A free and equal representation is the best, if not the only foundation upon which a free government can be built; and, consequently, that the greatest care should be taken in laying it. I am, sir, one of the people; such I shall continue; and, with their feelings, I hold "that the right of electing persons to represent the people in the federal government, is an important and sacred right." 

The opinions that have been offered upon the manner in which the exercise of this right is provided for by the fourth section, satisfies me that we are all [[☃ definition 1]] for the same [[☃ definition 2]], and that we only differ as to the means of attaining it. For my own part, I confess that I prize the fourth section as highly as any in the Constitution. I consider the [[☃ definition 3]] of the national government, the branch chosen immediately for the people, as intended to be a check on the [[☃ definition 4]], which is not an immediate representation of the people of America, and is not chosen by them, but is a representation of the [[☃ definition 5]] of the individual states, and its members delegated by the several state legislatures. 

**Which statement best summarizes Cabot’s argument about the Elections Clause?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gives the state governments the power to monitor and change federal election processes, ensuring free and fair elections.
It preserves state power over the federal government, allowing state legislatures to pick the representatives in both chambers of Congress.
It guarantees that all people have the right to vote in all levels of elections, supporting a government built on the consent of the governed.
It protects the people’s right to choose their representatives in the House, curbing the power of state governments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from notes taken during the debate at the Massachusetts Ratifying Convention in 1788. The speaker is Theophilus Parsons, and he is speaking about the Elections Clause.**

But a state legislature, under the influence of their senators, who would have their fullest confidence, or under the influence of ambitious or popular characters, or in times of popular commotion, and when faction and party spirit run high, would introduce such regulations as would [[☃ definition 1]] the rights of the people [[☃ definition 2]] and of little value. They might make an unequal and partial division of the State into districts for the election of representatives, or they might even disqualify one-third of the electors. Without these powers in Congress, the people can have no remedy.

**According to Parsons, how does the Elections Clause check the powers of state governments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gives Congress the power to oversee elections and prevent state legislatures from making unfair election rules, protecting the people’s voting rights.
It allows for the direct election of members of the House, ensuring that the people have power over one chamber of the federal legislature.
It guarantees that the judicial branch has the power to review any election laws passed by the states and overturn them if they violate people’s voting rights.
It allows the federal government to appoint the state representatives who make the election laws, providing for free and fair elections.
